Name: spectral_latency_aware_inference
Title: Spectral Latency-Aware Inference: Training-Free Test-Time Optimization via Norm-Preserving Subspace Projection
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral latent steering can be used to achieve training-free test-time optimization while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry and enforcing strict computational budgets.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works in spectral quantization routing, geometric interference cancellation, and spectral signal separation. However, our approach differs in that it focuses on training-free test-time optimization via norm-preserving subspace projection, which has not been explored in the existing literature.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to training-free test-time optimization, leveraging spectral latent steering to restrict test-time updates to a low-rank, norm-preserving subspace. Our method, Spectral Latency-Aware Inference (SLAI), performs a single-pass PCA on initial layer activations to identify dominant spectral directions and projects subsequent test-time updates onto this subspace. We theoretically ground SLAI in information-theoretic bounds, demonstrating that low-rank projections capture the majority of gradient-aligned signal required for reasoning improvement. Empirically, we evaluate SLAI on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, showing that it matches or exceeds full-space perturbation baselines while reducing hidden-state perturbation overhead and enforcing deterministic latency bounds.
Experiments: Evaluate SLAI on mathematical reasoning tasks, comparing its performance to full-space perturbation baselines and measuring hidden-state perturbation overhead.
Assess SLAI on code generation tasks, examining its ability to enforce deterministic latency bounds and reduce computational budgets.
Investigate the effect of subspace dimensionality on SLAI's performance, exploring the trade-off between optimization landscape preservation and computational efficiency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of SLAI may depend on the quality of the initial layer activations and the choice of subspace dimensionality.
SLAI may not be suitable for tasks that require significant changes to the optimization landscape, as the norm-preserving subspace projection may limit the extent of test-time updates.
